name: Puerto de Tíscar Loop
name_original: Quesada – vuelta por Capilla del Cristo del Valle de Belerda
description: Cycle the difficult 22.7-mile Puerto de Tíscar Loop through the picturesque Andalusian countryside, gaining 3219 feet in elevation.
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Quesada offer diverse terrain, characterized by varying elevations, natural parks, and agricultural landscapes. The region features hilly areas, including views towards mountains like the Sierra de Cazorla, alongside flatter sections near the coast and around natural lagoons. Cyclists can navigate through orange groves and explore routes that connect to coastal towns. This varied topography provides a range of road cycling experiences. Best road cycling routes around Quesada The most popular road cycling route is Puerto de Tíscar Loop, a 22.7 miles (36.6 km) trail that takes 2 hours 16 minutes to complete. This difficult route features significant elevation gain through scenic countryside. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is From the Guadiana Menor through the Cueva de la Virgen del Tiscar — circular, a moderate 17.7 miles (28.4 km) path. This route offers scenic landscapes and varying elevations. Local road cyclists also love the Cazorla – La Yedra Castle loop from La Cávila, a 29.1 miles (46.8 km) trail leading through hilly terrain with views of historic landmarks, often completed in about 3 hours 0 minutes. Road cycling around Quesada is defined by hilly terrain, natural parks, and agricultural landscapes. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate rides to challenging climbs. The routes in Quesada are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from over 10 reviews. More than 1,200 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Quesada's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Quesada?
A: There are over 50 road cycling routes around Quesada, offering a wide range of options for different skill levels. You'll find everything from moderate rides to challenging climbs through varied landscapes.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect when road cycling in Quesada?
A: Road cycling in Quesada offers diverse terrain. You'll encounter hilly areas with views towards mountains like the Sierra de Cazorla, alongside flatter sections near the coast and around natural lagoons. Routes often pass through picturesque orange groves and agricultural landscapes, providing a varied experience.

Q: Are there any challenging road cycling routes for experienced riders?
A: Yes, Quesada offers several challenging routes. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/24523815" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Yedra Castle – Cazorla loop from Quesada&lt;/a&gt; is an extensive 108 km ride with substantial elevation gain, perfect for experienced cyclists seeking a demanding tour. Another difficult option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38167918" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Quesada – Puerto de Tíscar loop from El Salón&lt;/a&gt;, covering over 78 km with significant climbing.

Q: Are there any moderate road cycling routes suitable for intermediate riders?
A: Absolutely. A popular moderate option is &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1345162102/from-the-guadiana-menor-through-the-cueva-de-la-virgen-del-tiscar-circular" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;From the Guadiana Menor through the Cueva de la Virgen del Tiscar — circular&lt;/a&gt;, a 28.4 km path offering scenic landscapes and varying elevations without being overly strenuous. There are 20 moderate routes in total to explore.

Q: What natural attractions or landmarks can I see along road cycling routes in Quesada?
A: Many routes offer stunning natural beauty and points of interest. You can cycle near the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4473525" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Malena Waterfall&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1062504" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Linarejos Waterfall&lt;/a&gt;. The region also features the striking Pink Laguna within the La Mata and Torrevieja Lagoons Natural Park, known for flamingos. You might also pass by historic sites like the Rojales Caves (Cuevas del Rodeo) or enjoy views from the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1752638" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto de las Palomas Viewpoint&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes in the area?
A: Yes, many routes are designed as loops. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e996238434/puerto-de-tiscar-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto de Tíscar Loop&lt;/a&gt; is a challenging 36.6 km circular route through scenic countryside. Another option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31861504" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cazorla – La Yedra Castle loop from La Cávila&lt;/a&gt;, a 46.8 km trail through hilly terrain.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Quesada?
A: Quesada benefits from a warm Mediterranean climate, making it an ideal year-round destination for cycling. Mild winters and hot summers mean you can enjoy the routes almost any time, though spring and autumn offer particularly pleasant temperatures for longer rides.

Q: Can I find family-friendly road cycling routes in Quesada?
A: While many routes involve varying elevations, there are options suitable for families. The Laguna de la Mata Loop, for example, is a relatively easy 25 km ride around the beautiful Laguna de la Mata Natural Park, which can be a good choice for a more relaxed family outing.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy most about the routes in Quesada?
A: The road cycling routes in Quesada are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 5.0 stars from over 10 reviews. Cyclists often praise the diverse terrain, from challenging climbs to scenic coastal rides, and the beautiful natural parks and agricultural landscapes that make each tour unique.

Q: Are there opportunities to cycle to the coast from Quesada?
A: Yes, Quesada is just a short drive from the stunning Blue Flag beaches of the Costa Blanca. You can find routes that lead to coastal towns like Guardamar del Segura or Torrevieja, offering a refreshing change of scenery and the chance to cycle along the Mediterranean Sea.

Q: Are there any specific natural parks or protected areas that road cyclists can explore?
A: Road cyclists can explore routes that pass through or near several significant natural areas. The Sierra de Cazorla, Segura y Las Villas Natural Park offers wild experiences, while the La Mata and Torrevieja Lagoons Natural Park is home to diverse flora and fauna, including the famous Pink Laguna. El Recorral Natural Park also provides picturesque landscapes and tranquil ponds.

Q: Are there any routes that follow rivers or offer waterside views?
A: Yes, scenic paths run along the Segura River, which can be followed by bike from Rojales (near Quesada) down to the beach of Guardamar. This offers a pleasant waterside cycling experience away from the more challenging hilly terrains.
tips:
Following the GR 247 path you will arrive at the port of Tíscar. This port separates the rugged landscapes full of pine forests from a more humanized environment.

As a curiosity, Tíscar means "pass between mountains" in the Berber language.
The Blue Pilon is between the villages of Don Pedro and Belerda. The waterfall is formed by the Tíscar river, which comes down from the incredible Cueva del Agua and creates this waterfall.

A town located in the southeast of the province, at the foot of the Sierra de Cazorla and facing the olive-growing countryside. Birthplace of the painter Rafael Zabaleta, to whom he has dedicated a museum with a large part of his work. It is a colorful collection of narrow streets with white houses in which the church of San Pedro and San Pablo, the church of the hospital, the Arco de la Manquita de Utrera and the Arco de Los Santos or del Señor stand out.

faqs.question: Is the Puerto de Tíscar Loop suitable for beginner road cyclists?
faqs.answer: This route is categorized as a "difficult" road cycling experience, demanding "very good fitness" due to its significant elevation gain of nearly 1000 meters over approximately 36.6 km. It is generally not recommended for beginners.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on the Puerto de Tíscar Loop?
faqs.answer: The route is primarily composed of mostly paved surfaces, offering a classic road cycling experience. However, be aware that some segments may be unpaved and could be challenging to ride, so a robust road bike or gravel bike might be advantageous.

faqs.question: What natural and historical features can I see along the route?
faqs.answer: You'll be immersed in the picturesque Andalusian countryside, with panoramic views of local landscapes. The route passes through the town of Quesada, known for its white houses, and includes the challenging climb to Puerto de Tíscar. You might also catch glimpses of the Pilón Azul Waterfall.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle the Puerto de Tíscar Loop?
faqs.answer: The route can be enjoyed for much of the year. The abundance of trees along the road to Puerto de Tíscar provides ample shade, making it more pleasant during warmer periods. However, to avoid the peak heat of summer, it's advisable to cycle before noon.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the Puerto de Tíscar Loop?
faqs.answer: The town of Quesada often serves as a starting point for this loop. You can typically find parking within or on the outskirts of the town, which is nestled in the foothills of the Sierra de Cazorla.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or fees required to cycle this route in the Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y las Villas Natural Park?
faqs.answer: Generally, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required for cycling on public roads within the Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y las Villas Natural Park. However, always respect local regulations, stay on marked paths, and be mindful of wildlife and the natural environment.

faqs.question: Is the Puerto de Tíscar Loop dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While the route is primarily a road cycling path, dogs are generally allowed in the natural park area, often on a leash. If you plan to bring a dog, ensure they are comfortable with road cycling environments and that you adhere to all local leash laws and clean-up regulations.

faqs.question: Are there any specific tips for cycling this route during warmer months?
faqs.answer: Yes, the road leading to Puerto de Tíscar is notable for its ample natural shade provided by numerous trees. This makes it more comfortable during hotter months in Andalusia. To maximize comfort, it's highly recommended to start your ride before noon, especially in summer.

faqs.question: Does the Puerto de Tíscar Loop intersect with other cycling paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route incorporates sections of several other paths. You will cycle along parts of [CIMA JA05] Puerto de Tíscar * Huesa / Belerda, [CIMA JA05] Puerto de Tíscar * Quesada, and Via Pecuaria del Vadillo a la Presilla, among others.

faqs.question: Are there places to find refreshments or water along the route?
faqs.answer: The town of Quesada, a common starting point, offers amenities like cafes and shops where you can stock up on water and snacks. While the route itself is scenic, services might be limited between settlements, so it's wise to carry sufficient water and provisions.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete the Puerto de Tíscar Loop?
faqs.answer: For an average cyclist, this challenging 36.6 km route with significant elevation gain typically takes around 2 hours and 16 minutes to complete. This duration can vary based on your fitness level and how often you stop to enjoy the scenery.
